\title{\textbf{\large Constant-memory out-of-core single-cell preprocessing to one million cells with anndataoom}}
\author[1,2]{Zehua Zeng}
\author[1]{anndata-oom contributors}
\affil[1]{OmicVerse Project}
\affil[2]{\normalfont\texttt{starlitnightly@163.com}\ \textbar\ \texttt{https://github.com/omicverse/anndata-oom}}
\date{}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
\noindent
Single-cell atlases now routinely exceed a million cells, but the standard analysis stack loads the entire expression matrix into memory, so peak RAM grows linearly with cell count and crashes commodity hardware. We present anndataoom, a Rust-backed out-of-memory AnnData store that keeps the matrix on disk and runs the omicverse preprocessing pipeline (quality control, normalization, scaling and principal component analysis) as lazy, chunked transforms, so peak memory is bounded by the chunk size rather than the number of cells. Across a Tabula Sapiens series spanning 5,000 to 1,053,033 cells under a 256 GB cap, anndataoom holds peak resident memory essentially flat at roughly 0.9 to 5.0 GB, whereas in-memory and scanpy-backed configurations grow memory linearly and are out-of-memory-killed beyond 228,032 cells. At the largest point where every configuration survives, anndataoom uses about 53x less memory than dense in-memory processing, and it is the only configuration that completes the million-cell dataset, finishing in 44.8 min at 5.0 GB peak. Wall-clock time tracks the in-memory band beyond the smallest dataset, and an optional GPU-mixed path is memory- and runtime-invariant. anndataoom delivers constant-memory, in-place, API-compatible out-of-core preprocessing at atlas scale on a workstation.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}
Single-cell genomics has entered the atlas era, with reference resources such as Tabula Sapiens \cite{tabulasapiens2022} and the CZ CELLxGENE Discover corpus \cite{megill2021cellxgene,czi2025cellxgene} aggregating tens to hundreds of millions of cells. The de facto data structure for these analyses is \texttt{AnnData} \cite{virshup2024anndata}, which organizes a central observations-by-variables expression matrix \texttt{X} together with aligned annotations, and the de facto algorithmic stack is built around \textsc{scanpy} \cite{wolf2018scanpy} and integrative frameworks such as omicverse \cite{omicverse}. A canonical preprocessing pipeline---quality control, total-count normalization, log transformation, highly variable gene selection, scaling, and principal component analysis (PCA)---transforms raw counts into the low-dimensional embedding on which clustering, trajectory inference and visualization depend.

The central scalability obstacle is that this stack is fundamentally in-memory: \texttt{X} is materialized in RAM, so peak memory grows linearly with the number of cells. A dense million-cell matrix over the full gene set requires on the order of $10^2$ GB, beyond the capacity of typical workstations and many shared compute nodes. As a result, the dataset size an analyst can process is dictated by available RAM rather than by the intrinsic cost of the computation.

Existing out-of-core strategies each relax this constraint at a cost. \texttt{AnnData}'s native backed mode reads \texttt{X} from disk without full materialization, but it can write back only \texttt{X}, loads \texttt{obs}/\texttt{var} eagerly, and frequently spills sparse data back into memory during processing. The experimental lazy-reading and Zarr-v3 facilities introduced in recent \texttt{AnnData} releases \cite{anndata012blog2025,anndataZarrV3Docs} back arrays with Dask and \texttt{xarray}, but \texttt{AnnData} core implements no preprocessing algorithms; those live in \textsc{scanpy}, where Dask support is partial and explicitly experimental---normalization and \texttt{log1p} are lazy, but highly variable gene selection and PCA force computation, and quality-control and scaling are not demonstrated as Dask-backed \cite{scanpyDaskTutorial}. Consequently the full quality-control-to-PCA pipeline is not end-to-end out-of-core in the standard stack. Alternative ecosystems shift the burden elsewhere: TileDB-SOMA and CELLxGENE Census \cite{tiledbsoma2026,czi2025cellxgene} require adopting a new store and streaming-query API rather than mutating \texttt{AnnData} in place; GPU frameworks such as rapids-singlecell \cite{dicks2026rapidssinglecell} and ScaleSC \cite{hu2025scalesc} demand NVIDIA hardware and, at the largest scales, multi-GPU clusters; and the Rust fully-backed \texttt{anndata-rs} powering SnapATAC2 \cite{anndatars,zhang2024snapatac2} is oriented toward single-cell epigenomics rather than general scRNA-seq preprocessing.

We introduce \textbf{anndataoom} \cite{anndataoom}, an out-of-memory \texttt{AnnData} backend built on \texttt{anndata-rs} \cite{anndatars} that fills the underserved niche of a true in-place, full-read/write, out-of-core engine that keeps the \textsc{scanpy}/omicverse API and standard on-disk stores. anndataoom keeps \texttt{X} on disk and composes each preprocessing step as a lazy transform descriptor---per-cell size factors for normalization, a flag for \texttt{log1p}, a column index for gene selection, mean and standard-deviation vectors for scaling---that is evaluated on-the-fly during chunked reads. Normalization statistics are accumulated by a single-pass numerically stable update \cite{welford1962numerically}, and PCA is computed by randomized SVD \cite{halko2011randomsvd} expressed as chunked matrix products, so the only in-memory products are the size-$k$ working set and the final embedding. Peak RAM is therefore bounded by the chunk size, not the cell count.

We benchmark anndataoom against in-memory and scanpy-backed configurations of the omicverse pipeline across a Tabula Sapiens cell-count series from $5{,}000$ to $\oneMCells$ cells under a $\rssCapGB$ GB resident-memory cap. The headline result is constant-memory scaling: anndataoom holds peak memory roughly flat across the entire range while every in-memory alternative grows linearly and is out-of-memory-killed at atlas scale, yet anndataoom completes the full pipeline at near-identical wall-clock time, trading a small fixed startup cost for more than $50\times$ memory savings and the ability to run datasets that crash every in-memory alternative.

\section{Related work}
\paragraph{The AnnData data model and its native out-of-core facilities.}
\texttt{AnnData} \cite{virshup2024anndata} is the standard container for single-cell data, organizing a central matrix \texttt{X} alongside aligned annotations (\texttt{obs}, \texttt{var}, \texttt{obsm}, \texttt{varm}, \texttt{obsp}, \texttt{varp}, \texttt{layers}, \texttt{uns}) and persisting to chunked HDF5 (\texttt{.h5ad}) and Zarr stores. It offers a backed out-of-memory mode and, more recently, experimental lazy reading via \texttt{read\_lazy}/\texttt{read\_elem\_lazy} and full Zarr-v3 support with sharding and pluggable cloud/GPU backends \cite{anndata012blog2025,anndataZarrV3Docs,anndataConcatOnDiskDocs}. Crucially, these facilities address I/O, lazy materialization, subsetting and on-disk concatenation only; \texttt{AnnData} core implements no preprocessing algorithms. Backed mode is also limited in practice: only \texttt{X} is written back, \texttt{obs}/\texttt{var} are read eagerly, and sparse reads can still spill to RAM. anndataoom instead provides a fully in-place backend in which preprocessing operations are evaluated against on-disk \texttt{X} through chunked, lazy transforms.

\paragraph{Distributed and lazy preprocessing in the scanpy stack.}
\textsc{scanpy} \cite{wolf2018scanpy} supplies the preprocessing algorithms and offers experimental Dask support \cite{scanpyDaskTutorial}: \texttt{normalize\_total} and \texttt{log1p} are lazy, but highly variable gene selection and PCA trigger computation, and quality-control metrics and scaling are not demonstrated as Dask-backed. The documentation explicitly warns that Dask support is new, highly experimental, and safe only on documented functions, with manual chunk and worker tuning. The end-to-end quality-control-to-PCA pipeline is therefore not out-of-core in this stack. anndataoom targets exactly this pipeline as a single constant-memory path while preserving the omicverse \cite{omicverse} \texttt{ov.pp} surface.

\paragraph{New-store and GPU approaches.}
TileDB-SOMA \cite{tiledbsoma2026} and CZ CELLxGENE Discover Census \cite{czi2025cellxgene,megill2021cellxgene} demonstrate out-of-core access to tens of millions of cells, including whole-corpus statistics on an 8 GB laptop, but require adopting a non-\texttt{AnnData} store and a streaming-query API; slices are converted to \texttt{AnnData} rather than mutated in place. GPU frameworks deliver large speedups: rapids-singlecell \cite{dicks2026rapidssinglecell} is a near-drop-in CuPy-backed \textsc{scanpy} replacement that now scales past a single GPU via multi-GPU Dask and managed-memory spilling, while ScaleSC \cite{hu2025scalesc} chunks along cells to reach 10--20M cells on a single A100 and circumvents the 32-bit indexing overflow that makes rapids-singlecell fail near 1.3M cells. Both require NVIDIA hardware. anndataoom is complementary: it is CPU-first and hardware-agnostic, and our benchmarks include an optional CPU--GPU-mixed path.

\paragraph{Rust-backed and curation layers.}
\texttt{anndata-rs} \cite{anndatars} provides a fully-backed HDF5 \texttt{AnnData} that stays synchronized with disk, opens at near-zero memory, and processes matrices by chunk; it ships inside SnapATAC2 \cite{zhang2024snapatac2}, which analyzes hundreds of thousands of cells in tens of GB but is oriented toward single-cell epigenomics. anndataoom builds on \texttt{anndata-rs} to deliver general scRNA-seq preprocessing. Orthogonally, data-management layers such as LaminDB \cite{laminlabs2025lamindb} and streaming dataloaders such as scDataset \cite{scdataset2025} govern or stream large corpora for machine-learning training rather than performing in-place interactive preprocessing. All implementations rely on the scientific Python stack \cite{virtanen2020scipy}.

\section{Results}
\paragraph{Benchmark design.}
We swept the omicverse \texttt{ov.pp} pipeline (load, quality control, preprocess, scale, PCA) across a seven-point Tabula Sapiens \cite{tabulasapiens2022} cell-count series (Table~\ref{tab:datasets}), from TS-5k ($5{,}000$ cells) to TS-1M ($\oneMCells$ cells, $60{,}606$ genes), under a $\rssCapGB$ GB resident-set-size (RSS) cap. Six configurations were compared: omicverse in-memory dense (\texttt{ov-anndata}), in-memory implicit-dense, scanpy backed mode (\texttt{backed='r'}), anndataoom (\texttt{ov-oom}), and the GPU-mixed variants of the out-of-memory and dense backends. Full per-dataset wall-clock and peak-RSS measurements are reported in Table~\ref{tab:headline}.

\paragraph{Constant-memory scaling is the headline result.}
Peak memory is the decisive axis (Fig.~\ref{fig:totaltime}b). The two anndataoom curves (CPU and GPU-mixed) coincide almost exactly and stay essentially flat at roughly $0.9$--$5.0$ GB across the entire $5{,}000$-to-$\oneMCells$ range, while the in-memory and scanpy-backed configurations rise with unit slope, growing memory linearly with cell count. At TS-Epi ($\fourWayCells$ cells), the largest point where all four in-memory/backed configurations still survive, peak RSS was $\fourWayRssAnn$ GB for in-memory dense, $\fourWayRssImp$ GB for the implicit-dense variant and $\fourWayRssBack$ GB for scanpy-backed, versus only $\fourWayRssOom$ GB for anndataoom---an approximately $\largeMemRatio\times$ memory reduction over dense in-memory processing. Beyond this point the in-memory configurations are progressively out-of-memory-killed: dense, scanpy-backed and the dense GPU-mixed variant fail at the first larger dataset, TS-Immune ($\firstOomSize$ cells; the implicit-dense variant survives there at roughly $\implicitUnblockSize$ cells but is killed at TS-1M), whereas only the two anndataoom curves complete the full series. At TS-1M ($\oneMCells$ cells) anndataoom finishes in $\oneMTotalMin$ min at a peak of $\oneMPeakGB$ GB. The coincidence of the two anndataoom curves in both panels demonstrates mode-invariance: adding the GPU-mixed path changes neither memory footprint nor runtime.

\paragraph{Wall-clock time tracks the in-memory band beyond the smallest dataset.}
On end-to-end time (Fig.~\ref{fig:totaltime}a), five of six configurations collapse onto a single near-linear log-log band; anndataoom pays a fixed startup overhead that makes it the slowest only at TS-5k, after which its curve converges with the in-memory band from TS-Vasc onward. scanpy-backed is the consistent time outlier, running roughly $2\times$ faster at small and mid scale (for example, $375.8$ s at TS-Epi versus $\sim\!800$--$924$ s for the other surviving configurations), but it cannot reach the large datasets and is killed at TS-Immune. The per-stage decomposition (Fig.~\ref{fig:stages}) shows preprocess as the dominant and fastest-growing stage, eventually swamping the others, while load and quality control shrink in relative share as data grows; anndataoom's larger load/setup component at TS-5k accounts for its small-data overhead. At atlas scale this overhead is negligible: PCA alone on the million-cell dataset completed in $171$ s ($2.8$ min). At TS-Immune and TS-1M, only the anndataoom bars are present; the in-memory and backed bars are replaced by OOM markers.

\paragraph{The CPU--GPU-mixed path is break-even.}
A direct comparison of CPU versus CPU--GPU-mixed execution (Fig.~\ref{fig:mixed}, Table~\ref{tab:mixed}) shows the two curves essentially superimposed for both backends, with mixed-mode speedups clustering tightly around unity (anndataoom: $0.96$--$1.08$, aside from a $1.51$ spike at TS-5k dominated by warm-up noise on a tiny dataset; in-memory dense: $0.90$--$1.01$). GPU memory remained at $0$ MB for all anndataoom mixed runs, indicating that PCA was not actually GPU-offloaded in these stages, whereas the dense mixed runs used $324$--$3{,}799$ MB of GPU memory for no consistent time benefit. At TS-1M the mixed run took $\mixedOomMixSec$ s versus $\mixedOomCpuSec$ s for CPU (speedup $\mixedOomSpeedup$), at $\mixedOomMixRssGB$ GB versus $\mixedOomCpuRssGB$ GB peak RSS---confirming that enabling the GPU path neither raises memory nor changes runtime for the out-of-memory backend.

\paragraph{Pipeline coverage.}
Of the $\compatNFuncs$ \texttt{ov.pp} functions surveyed, $\compatNOk$ run on the out-of-memory backend (Table~\ref{tab:compat}). Fourteen are \emph{constant-memory chunked-native}: the full quality-control-to-PCA pipeline plus neighbours, Leiden/Louvain, UMAP, t-SNE, MDE, normalization, \texttt{log1p} and robust-gene identification, with neighbours, UMAP and MDE GPU-capable. The remaining eight---highly variable feature selection, Pearson-residual normalization, regression, Scrublet, cell-cycle scoring, and the gene/cell filters, which densify \texttt{X} or perform eager variable lookups---are supported through a \emph{materialise-and-run} guard: an \texttt{AnnDataOOM} passed to one of these is transparently converted to in-memory, the function runs unchanged under its original name, and any new annotations are copied back to the out-of-memory object. This keeps the entire \texttt{ov.pp} surface callable on an \texttt{AnnDataOOM}; the guarded path is correct and API-identical but bounded by the per-call materialised footprint rather than constant-memory. Only the two \texttt{rapids\_singlecell}-dependent GPU converters (\texttt{anndata\_to\_GPU}/\texttt{anndata\_to\_CPU}) are unsupported here---an optional-dependency matter rather than a backend limitation---and \texttt{sude} is unstable in mixed mode (NaN).

\section{Discussion}
\paragraph{Summary.}
anndataoom makes peak memory a function of chunk size rather than cell count, turning the dataset-size ceiling from a hardware constraint into a near-constant. Across a $5{,}000$-to-$\oneMCells$-cell Tabula Sapiens series under a $\rssCapGB$ GB cap, it held peak RSS essentially flat at roughly $0.9$--$5.0$ GB, used about $\largeMemRatio\times$ less memory than dense in-memory processing at the largest commonly survivable dataset, and was the only configuration to complete the million-cell pipeline ($\oneMTotalMin$ min, $\oneMPeakGB$ GB peak) while every in-memory and backed alternative was out-of-memory-killed beyond $228{,}032$ cells. Critically, this memory saving came at near-identical wall-clock time, trading only a small fixed startup cost. Because preprocessing steps are encoded as lazy transform descriptors over on-disk \texttt{X} and PCA is computed by chunked randomized SVD \cite{halko2011randomsvd} with single-pass statistics \cite{welford1962numerically}, the backend remains a drop-in within the omicverse \cite{omicverse} and \texttt{AnnData} \cite{virshup2024anndata} ecosystem rather than a separate store.

\paragraph{Positioning.}
anndataoom occupies a niche left open by existing approaches: a true in-place, full-read/write out-of-core backend that keeps the \textsc{scanpy}/omicverse API and standard on-disk stores, without forcing a new store and query API (TileDB-SOMA/Census \cite{tiledbsoma2026,czi2025cellxgene}), GPUs (rapids-singlecell, ScaleSC \cite{dicks2026rapidssinglecell,hu2025scalesc}), the experimental and partial Dask path \cite{scanpyDaskTutorial}, or an epigenomics-oriented Rust stack \cite{zhang2024snapatac2}. It is complementary to all of these: the constant-memory CPU engine can serve as the substrate that feeds GPU acceleration or curation layers \cite{laminlabs2025lamindb} when that hardware or governance is available.

\paragraph{Limitations and outlook.}
The \emph{constant-memory} envelope covers the fourteen chunked-native functions; the eight densifying or lookup-heavy steps (Pearson-residual normalization, regression, Scrublet, cell-cycle scoring, the filters and the standalone HVG variants) are callable but run through the materialise-and-run guard, so they are bounded by the per-call in-memory footprint rather than constant-memory, and \texttt{sude} is unstable in mixed mode. Promoting these guarded steps to chunked-native implementations is the natural next step toward an end-to-end constant-memory surface. In the configurations tested, the GPU-mixed path was break-even and did not actually offload PCA (GPU memory stayed at $0$ MB for anndataoom), so realizing GPU speedups for the chunked SVD is a further avenue for future work, alongside exploiting Zarr-v3 sharded and remote stores \cite{anndataZarrV3Docs} for cloud-native execution. More broadly, by decoupling processable dataset size from RAM, anndataoom lets a commodity workstation run preprocessing pipelines that previously required large-memory servers, lowering the hardware barrier to atlas-scale single-cell analysis.

\section{Methods}

\paragraph{Storage and transform layer.} The h5ad reader is the
\texttt{anndata-rs} Rust library\cite{anndatars}, exposed to Python as a
\texttt{BackedArray} proxy that yields \texttt{scipy.sparse} chunks without
densifying on read. Per-cell normalisation is left multiplication by a sparse
diagonal of inverse size factors; $\log(1{+}x)$ is applied to the stored
values only (sparsity-preserving, since $\log(1{+}0)=0$). Per-gene mean and
variance are computed in a single chunked pass with a hybrid estimator:
per-chunk statistics use the sparse-native $E[X^2]-E[X]^2$ identity and
cross-chunk accumulation uses Welford's method\cite{welford1962numerically},
keeping the error at the \texttt{float32}$\to$\texttt{float64} floor
($\sim$$7\times10^{-8}$ relative).

\paragraph{Chunked PCA.} Dimensionality reduction uses randomised
SVD\cite{halko2011randomsvd} over the lazy scaled matrix. When the post-HVG
matrix fits a configurable budget it is built once into RAM and factorised in
a single pass; otherwise an implicit-centered formulation rewrites the
matrix--vector products through
$(N-\mu)/\sigma\cdot W = N\cdot\mathrm{diag}(1/\sigma)\cdot W - (\mu/\sigma)\cdot W$,
where $N$ is the sparse normalise+$\log(1{+}x)$ view, so no dense per-chunk
block is allocated. When materialising, HVG columns are sliced from the
sparse parent \emph{before} densifying---normalisation is per-row and
$\log(1{+}x)$ element-wise, so the column slice commutes through both---so only
a $(\mathrm{chunk}\times n_\mathrm{HVG})$ block is ever dense, numerically
identical to densifying the full panel (maximum absolute difference
$\sim$$10^{-7}$).

\paragraph{Benchmark configurations.} Four CPU storage/scaling backends:
\texttt{ov-anndata} (\texttt{anndata.read\_h5ad}; \texttt{ov.pp.scale}
densifies the full panel); \texttt{ov-anndata-implicit} (implicit-centered
scaled wrapper); \texttt{scanpy-backed} (\texttt{backed='r'}, then the
standard scanpy pipeline---the first quality-control op has no backed handler,
so the workflow materialises to memory before QC, leaving the backed
advantage only at load); and \texttt{ov-oom} (\texttt{anndataoom.read};
operations dispatch to the chunked path via \texttt{is\_oom}). Two
\texttt{cpu-gpu-mixed} variants (\texttt{ov-oom-mixed},
\texttt{ov-anndata-mixed}) run the identical pipeline with
PCA/neighbours/UMAP offloaded to a GPU via PyTorch.

\paragraph{Datasets and pipeline.} Seven Tabula Sapiens
slices\cite{tabulasapiens2022} from cellxgene\cite{megill2021cellxgene} on the
$60{,}606$-gene panel: TS-Vasculature ($42{,}650$) and two seed-0 subsamples
($5{,}000$/$10{,}000$); the stromal ($232{,}684$), epithelial ($228{,}032$)
and immune ($592{,}317$) compartments; and a one-million-cell concatenation
($\oneMCells$). Each uses \texttt{layers["decontXcounts"]} as \texttt{.X}; the
harness promotes the raw-counts layer when \texttt{.X} appears
log-normalised. The pipeline is \texttt{ov.pp.qc} (mode \texttt{seurat},
doublets off), \texttt{ov.pp.preprocess} (\texttt{shiftlog$\vert$pearson},
$2{,}000$ HVGs), \texttt{ov.pp.scale} (\texttt{max\_value}=10) and
\texttt{ov.pp.pca} (50 components), all on one consistent software stack.

\paragraph{Measurement.} Per-stage wall-clock and RSS are captured around each
stage (peak RSS = maximum post-stage RSS). The main sweep runs on a single CPU
node under a $\rssCapGB$~GB per-process cap; the GPU-mixed comparison runs on
an NVIDIA H100, additionally recording per-stage peak PyTorch device memory.
Numerical equivalence between backends is quantified by per-component absolute
cosine similarity.

\paragraph{Code and data availability.} The harness, figure/table generators
and dataset cards are at the project repository\cite{anndataoom}. Subsamples
use a fixed seed; the million-cell input is the row-concatenation of the three
compartment slices.
